sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=46).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 30, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 7 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the bottom panels more clearly. The ascii row 58 is:
BBBBBBBgYYBWBWBgBbBBBWBBBbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBB
Let me parse the bottom panels:
- Gray panel: cols 7-15 (gYYBWBWBgB)
  - g at col 7
  - YY at cols 8-9
  - B at col 10
  - W at col 11
  - B at col 12
  - W at col 13
  - B at col 14
  - g at col 15
- Blue panel: cols 17-25 (bBBBWBBBb)
  - b at col 17
  - BBB at cols 18-20
  - W at col 21
  - BBB at cols 22-24
  - b at col 25
Wait, but the segmentation shows the panels are at different positions. Let me look at the segmentation data for the bottom panels.
Actually, looking at the B/b cells, I see:
- Gray panel: cols 3-16 (with g at edges)
- Blue panel: cols 17-25 (with b at edges)
But the ascii shows the gray panel starts at col 7 (g) and the blue panel starts at col 17 (b).
Let me re-examine. The B cells at cols 3-6 and 16 might be part of the panel border or background.
Looking at the segmentation, the bottom panels are:
- Gray panel: cols ~4-14 (based on earlier analysis)
- Blue panel: cols ~15-25 (based on earlier analysis)
But the current ascii shows:
- g at col 7 (gray panel start)
- g at col 15 (gray panel end)
- b at col 17 (blue panel start)
- b at col 25 (blue panel end)
So the gray panel is at cols 7-15, and the blue panel is at cols 17-25.
The B cells within the gray panel are at cols 10, 12, 14.
The B cells within the blue panel are at cols 18-20, 22-24.
The W cells are at cols 11, 13 (gray panel) and col 21 (blue panel).
The actions that worked were:
- MOUSE(58, 10) -> col 10 is a B cell on the gray panel
- MOUSE(58, 21) -> col 21 is a W cell on the blue panel
Wait, col 21 is a W cell, not a b cell. Let me re-check.
Row 58: BBBBBBBgYYBWBWBgBbBBBWBBBbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Col 0-6: BBBBBBB
- Col 7: g
- Col 8-9: YY
- Col 10: B
- Col 11: W
- Col 12: B
- Col 13: W
- Col 14: B
- Col 15: g
- Col 16: B
- Col 17: b
- Col 18-20: BBB
- Col 21: W
- Col 22-24: BBB
- Col 25: b
- Col 26-29: BBBB
So MOUSE(58, 21) clicked col 21, which is a W cell on the blue panel. And MOUSE(58, 10) clicked col 10, which is a B cell on the gray panel.
Hmm, so the interactive cells might be the B cells on the gray panel and the W cell on the blue panel.
But wait, the transition data shows:
- Transition 23: MOUSE(58, 10) -> pattern moved from (16, 22) to (16, 6)
- Transition 27: MOUSE(58, 21) -> pattern moved from (16, 6) to (8, 14)
And the transitions that didn't work:
- Transition 26: MOUSE(58, 11) -> no change (col 11 is a W cell on the gray panel)
So clicking a W cell on the gray panel didn't work, but clicking a B cell on the gray panel did work. And clicking a W cell on the blue panel did work.
This suggests that:
- On the gray panel, the B cells are interactive
- On the blue panel, the W cell is interactive
Let me try clicking the other B cells on the gray panel (cols 12 and 14) to see what happens.
Actually, I realize I should focus on the goal. The pattern is at (8, 14), and I want to move it to (24, 14). That's down by 16 rows.
Looking at the previous movements:
- MOUSE(58, 10) -> moved left by 16 columns
- MOUSE(58, 21) -> moved up by 8 rows, right by 8 columns
If I click MOUSE(58, 21) again, will it move the pattern down by 8 rows? Or will it move it up by another 8 rows?
Let me try clicking MOUSE(58, 21) to see what happens. If it moves the pattern down, great. If it moves it up, I need to find another button.
Actually, I should also try clicking the other B cells on the gray panel to see if they produce different movements.
Let me try MOUSE(58, 12) and MOUSE(58, 14) to see what they do.
But first, let me think about the efficiency. I've used 7 actions so far, and I need to be efficient. Let me try to figure out the button mapping before making more moves.
Looking at the gray panel B cells:
- Col 10: moved left by 16
- Col 12: unknown
- Col 14: unknown
Looking at the blue panel W cell:
- Col 21: moved up by 8, right by 8
The movements seem to be:
- Left by 16 (col 10)
- Up by 8, right by 8 (col 21)
If the buttons produce directional movements, then:
- Col 10: left
- Col 12: ?
- Col 14: ?
- Col 21: up-right
Maybe the buttons produce movements in different directions:
- Col 10: left
- Col 12: up
- Col 14: down
- Col 21: up-right (or some other direction)
Or maybe:
- Col 10: left
- Col 12: right
- Col 14: down
- Col 21: up
Let me try clicking MOUSE(58, 14) to see if it moves the pattern down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking the rightmost B cell on the gray panel to see if it moves down
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 14}])
print(f"Action result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Check the new pattern position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 14:
        col = n['boundary'][0][1]
        if col < 32:
            print(f"Pattern now at: ({n['boundary'][0][0]}, {n['boundary'][0][1]})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Action result: board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True
Pattern now at: (16, 6)
